entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub.

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment

entry.title: house-sales-competition-1
entry.content:
You have some experience with R or Python and machine learning basics. This is a perfect competition for data science students who have completed an online course in machine learning and are looking to expand their skill set before trying a featured competition.
Housing Prices Competition
Category: Kaggle competition
Text:
This competition is for beginners and it is slightly easier than the ongoing competitions with rewards.
It requires competitors who have learned about R or python to gain some experience in solving this problem. The target is to predict the given data on house sales prices. Root Mean Squared Error is used for the accuracy of the predictions. In the final submission of predictions, each player should hand out a format with the Id and its corresponding SalePrice.
To help the starters better understand this competition, Kaggle also linked with relatively machine learning lessons and some R information.
The dataset description part shows files might be useful. In the data description, it explains the abbreviations of each column’s name of the dataset, which is helpful and necessary for others to understand. All descriptions are as follows:
• SalePrice - the property’s sale price in dollars. This is the target variable that you’re trying to predict.
• MSSubClass: The building class
• MSZoning: The general zoning classification
• LotFrontage: Linear feet of street connected to the property
• LotArea: Lot size in square feet
• Street: Type of road access
• Alley: Type of alley access
• LotShape: General shape of the property
• LandContour: Flatness of the property
• Utilities: Type of utilities available
• LotConfig: Lot configuration
• LandSlope: Slope of property
• Neighborhood: Physical locations within Ames city limits
• Condition1: Proximity to main road or railroad
• Condition2: Proximity to the main road or railroad (if a second is present)
• BldgType: Type of dwelling
• HouseStyle: Style of dwelling
• OverallQual: Overall material and finish quality
• OverallCond: Overall condition rating
• YearBuilt: Original construction date
• YearRemodAdd: Remodel date
• RoofStyle: Type of roof
• RoofMatl: Roof material
• Exterior1st: Exterior covering on the house
• Exterior2nd: Exterior covering on house (if more than one material)
• MasVnrType: Masonry veneer type
• MasVnrArea: Masonry veneer area in square feet
• ExterQual: Exterior material quality
• ExterCond: Present condition of the material on the exterior
• Foundation: Type of foundation
• BsmtQual: Height of the basement
• BsmtCond: General condition of the basement
• BsmtExposure: Walkout or garden level basement walls
• BsmtFinType1: Quality of basement finished area
• BsmtFinSF1: Type 1 finished square feet
• BsmtFinType2: Quality of second finished area (if present)
• BsmtFinSF2: Type 2 finished square feet
• BsmtUnfSF: Unfinished square feet of basement area
• TotalBsmtSF: Total square feet of basement area
• Heating: Type of heating
• HeatingQC: Heating quality and condition
• CentralAir: Central air conditioning
• Electrical: Electrical system
• 1stFlrSF: First Floor square feet
• 2ndFlrSF: Second floor square feet
• LowQualFinSF: Low quality finished square feet (all floors)
• GrLivArea: Above grade (ground) living area square feet
• BsmtFullBath: Basement full bathrooms
• BsmtHalfBath: Basement half bathrooms
• FullBath: Full bathrooms above grade
• HalfBath: Half baths above grade
• Bedroom: Number of bedrooms above the basement level
• Kitchen: Number of kitchens
• KitchenQual: Kitchen Quality
• TotRmsAbvGrd: Total rooms above grade (does not include bathrooms)
• Functional: Home functionality rating
• Fireplaces: Number of fireplaces
• FireplaceQu: Fireplace quality
• GarageType: Garage location
• GarageYrBlt: Year the garage was built
• GarageFinish: Interior finish of the garage
• GarageCars: Size of garage in car capacity
• GarageArea: Size of garage in square feet
• GarageQual: Garage quality
• GarageCond: Garage condition
• PavedDrive: Paved driveway
• WoodDeckSF: Wood deck area in square feet
• OpenPorchSF: Open porch area in square feet
• EnclosedPorch: Enclosed porch area in square feet
• 3SsnPorch: Three-season porch area in square feet
• ScreenPorch: Screen porch area in square feet
• PoolArea: Pool area in square feet
• PoolQC: Pool quality
• Fence: Fence Quality
• MiscFeature: Miscellaneous feature not covered in other categories
• MiscVal: $Value of miscellaneous feature
• MoSold: Month Sold
• YrSold: Year Sold
• SaleType: Type of sale
• SaleCondition: Condition of sale
The features of the dataset are nearly the same because of the fundamental of machine learning. It is that in order to estimate the performance of the machine learning model on new data, we can separate the data into two parts: the training set and the testing set. The training dataset is used to fit the machine learning model while the test dataset is used to evaluate the effect of the previously generated model. Though the data from these two parts are almost different, they should have the same features, and the same variables on the fitted model, to check the performance of the model. We can derive the model by using R or python code, which is also the main task of the competition.
The sample submission file is an example of the submission form which only has two columns: Id and SalePrice.
Problem Solving:
This competition is also a good application of knowledge about machine learning. In general, the original dataset contains some features we want but may miss some data as well. Facing real-world datasets, we need to master the skills of dealing with those problems, such as missing values, and categorical variables. We need to distinguish between numeric and alphabetic variables and then use a different method to classify and handle them.
In the advanced sections of machine learning, we have many tools to improve the performance of the model estimation results. By designing pipelines to compact the programming code and improve the quality, using cross-validation to improve the fitted model, building the XGBoost function to get a higher ranking in the game, and avoiding leakage, overfitting, or underfitting, we can improve our model in different side.
General logic:
Using python language
1- import the data:
Using pandas: read CSV command to import data; then use a model in the sklearn package. “train_test_split” command is to split one file into two parts and check the model by cross-validation. At this step, we can set different train sizes between 0 and 1, a numerical random state to divide the dataset in different ways.
In order to keep the useful features from the complete dataset, we can first set the features as what we want then copy the original files and limit the data in those columns. It is a useful way to reduce data while avoiding the influence of irrelevant factors on the model.
2- compare different models then keep the best model:
One of the most common models of the sklearn package is the Random Forest Model. The main idea of this regressor is by fitting a number of classifying decision trees on various subsamples of the dataset and using averages to improve the predictive accuracy while controlling over-fitting. Simply in this command, we can set different values of n_estimators (default=100), random_state, criterion (different function to measure the quality of a split e.g.: squared_error), max/min_depth/sample_split) … In the face of an unknown model, there are many parameters that we can decide on and change. In the beginning, we should try as many combinations of different initial settings as possible.
Recall the requirements of the competition, we need to use MAE to evaluate the accuracy of the model. We can import MAE from the sklearn.metrics. Since we create more than one model in the last step, it is easier to compare the result by defining a function that can be imported by model and parameters then printing out the absolute values and turning it to be readable and easy to compare.
After comparing the value of the function output, we can define the best model as the final model and take the same generate progress with the random forest regressor command to get the fitted value of the model.
3- Generate the output and submit
In the beginning, we define X and y from the original dataset, here we can use ‘model.fit(X,y)’ to fit the model and generate the test predictions by assigning the value equal to model.predict(X_test). The final step is to generate the output to a file by the ‘to csv’ command in pandas’ data frame package. For an unknown data set, it is relatively simple to get a model, but the difficulty lies in how to choose the method and adjust the parameters to obtain a more accurate model. The selection of models and the estimation of parameters will be the problems that every data analysis learner has to face.
Hope my notes can help you and we make progress together.

entry.title: house-sales-competition-2
entry.content:
Housing Prices Competition
Category: Kaggle competition
Text:
This article meanly introduces some important methods dealing with the real-world complicated dataset.
-1 Missing values
Not every column in the dataset has an exact value in the dataset. Those missing values show in the data as NaN which stands for not a number. Dealing with those missing values, the analysts can choose to drop those columns with missing values or get imputed values with some number generated by other samples. If we choose to add some imputation to fill the blank in the dataset, we can also add a new column remarking on the action of added imputed values.
First, we need to have a general understanding of the dataset. We can use ‘dataset.shape’ command to display the number of rows and columns. Besides ‘isnull’ command is specifically used to display missing values.
If we want to drop the columns with missing values, we can generate a new dataset with ‘.drop’ expression. In this step, col is short for columns in python language. Besides, in numpy and pandas, axis=0 stands for the actions to the horizontal axis while axis=1 refers to the vertical axis.
On the other hand, we can calculate the imputed value with the help of sklearn packages. One of the most simple ways is to enter the SimpleImputer command. There is another point that needs to be noticed. In the fitting process, we can use ‘fit_transform’ or ‘transform’. The former can finish both fits and transform: compute the mean and standard deviation for a given feature and also perform scaling using mean and standard deviation, the latter only works for transformation.
Same as before, we can calculate MAE from different approaches and choose the best method.
-2 Categorical variables
There are many non-numeric descriptive features in the common data set. Different from numerical characteristics which we can easily summarize by ‘describe()’ command. Generally speaking, the categorical variables have the following forms: words that indicate frequency, such as never, rarely, or every day, and nouns that describe characteristics, such as countries, brands, vehicles, etc.
For those objects, we want to compare and find the correlations among them which are the same as the numerical data.
One of the easiest ways to deal with them is to drop all the variables. However, removing useful information directly is a waste and also leads to inaccurate model estimation. As a result, this approach is not recommended.
Ordinal Encoding:
This method turns each unique value into a different integer. The size of the number also is defined logically. In order to solve the question, this integer reflects the relevance or importance. This programming method is usually used for turning an indisputable ranking into categories.
One-Hot Encoding
It creates new columns using a 0 or 1 to represent the absence or the presence of the categorical value. If the original data does not have some intrinsic ranking, it is a good way to transform it into nominal variables.
-3 Pipelines
In the common machine learning process, we use the train and test data and then generate an operationalizing model which could take a lot of time. Instead, pipelines are a useful way to not only compact your code and also organize the model.
There are many advantages to using pipelines. It can save time for repetitive work, improve the data-driven decision, and optimize strategies. For programmers, it provides cleaner code, fewer bugs, easier to produce, and also more options for model validation.
The sklearn packages provide us with some already-built functions in the pipeline approach. The same as the previous section, we can also call the column transformer, a simple imputer, one hot encoder function. After choosing the steps of the preprocessor and the model, we can fit the model and generate predictions and evaluate the model by calculating MAE.
To build a pipeline for the model, we can use the simple imputer to replace the missing values, call the random forest regressors to train a random forest model, then we can make a preliminary prediction. The number of trees in the random forest model is set by the n_estimators parameter, and the reproducibility is decided by random_state.
-4 Cross-Validation
Cross-validation is a useful way to measure the models’ performance. By cross-selecting the dataset, we can make the most use of it while avoiding over-fitting. If there is a large validation in the data, it displays less randomness which is harmful to the measure of model quality, so the result would be less reliable. After choosing the predictive variables to construct the model, we can use cross-validation to support the model.
The basic principle of cross-validation is that after dividing the data into training and validation parts, we change the role of each part to check the model’s quality. By repeating these steps, we can compare the result of the cross-validation and then get the best model.
The size of the dataset also helps analysts to decide whether to use cross-validation or not and how is the effect. For example, if the dataset is small, the calculation of extra computation is not very heavy, so it is suitable for this method. By contrast, a larger dataset is not guaranteed to save time and have a better result by using this measuring approach.
The cross-validation can also be called from the sklearn package. By comparing the score of cross-validation, with the help of MAE, we can measure the model quality and get a more convincing report.
In the corresponding exercise, we can calculate the average score of MAE by using cross-validation for the whole original dataset.
To construct a cross-validation evaluation model, we first need to define a function that can give scores to those different models. In this specific case, the error we want to be as small as possible. As a result, we can turn the error into a negative absolute error to fit the requirement in the scikit-learn convention, in which all metrics are evaluated as a higher number is better. The reason to take the average across value is that simply in one task we prefer to just compare one single measure of model quality. The next step is to test different parameter values. Setting while and for loop can make the process lean and intuitive. Then we can find out the best parameter value. As a matter of fact, the setting of n and the interval of the loop are equally important. For example, if we multiply i by 50, the interval is 50, and the result of n with best estimators is 200. However, the score of 205 is slightly smaller than 200. If we limit the calculation interval between 100 and 200, we won’t find out the value of 240 is better. How to set those parameters can not be ignored if we want to have a better solution.
-6 XGBoost
XGBoost can help us to build and optimize models with gradient boosting. Unlike the random forest model, XGBoost is an ensemble method combing the predictions of several models. It is initially generated with a single model, which prediction is also very simple. Then we can construct a cycle and add the content step by step. First, we use the current ensemble to generate predictions for each observation in the dataset and add more predictions from all models in the ensemble. Using these predictions we can have a loss function, which is used to fit a new model that later will be added to the ensemble. By repeating this, errors can be further reduced.
XGBoost stands for extreme gradient boosting, which is also an improvement in the performance and speed of gradient boosting. One approach to construct and fit the model is by using sklearn API for XGBoost. We will also learn more about tunable parameters. For example: n_estimators, early_stopping_rounds, learning_rate, n_jobs, etc. The most common parameter we might use is n_estimators. It defines how many times to go through the modeling cycle in the previous set. It is equal to the number of models in the ensemble. However, if we set the value too small or too large, it will cause the consequences of underfitting and overfitting. Therefore, it is necessary for us to learn more about how to avoid these mistakes.

entry.title: R-learning-notes-1
entry.content:
R learning notes 1
Category: R
Text:
R is a useful tool for data analysis. It is common when handling not only machine learning but also in academic fields, such as biology, astronomy in social science.
-1 General process
The typical analysis process is that
• Install and load packages: load the base packages, we can use ‘install.packages(‘’)’ or library(‘’) to achieve this.
• Load and prepare data: if we want to save the dataset to tibble (a package in R that is used to manipulate and print data frames) named “df” (data frame), ‘df <- ’. Besides, we have access to choose a part of the dataset to save and check the level of some specific column. mutate() adds new variables and preserves existing ones; transmute() adds new variables and drops existing ones.
• Show the outcome of variables: draw a bar chart of some characters, display frequency, filter out the NA elements, and charts of more than one element, and even draw metrics or divided the data by groups.
• Clean up: rm(list = ls()) to remove all objects from environment; p_unload(all) to remove all the packages; graphics.off() to clear all the plots and graphic devices; cat(“\014”) to clear the console window.
-2 Enter data:
Basic commands can solve the problem of basic math, and print number sequences or characters in the console. If we want to assign values to variables we should use <- instead of =. Multiple assignments work as well. The command c() means combining multiple values in a single variable. If we enter only variables, the contents will be printed in the console.
Numeric values are by default double precision. R does not distinguish a single character or collection of characters. Sequential data and metrics are supportable in R. However, the vector is set by default which does not have dimension.
Data types can be numeric, character, logical, and data structures like vectors, matrices, arrays, data frames, and lists. The List can be self-referential.
-3 Comments and headers:
With the sign #, we can temporarily turn off and on the code.
For example: if we want to draw a histogram, we can set the color, border, title, etc. With # temporarily appearing in the command, after re-running the whole command, it will change to the default settings or nothing.
A heading can help people to express the target more intuitively to others. There are three levels of headers in RStudio, they are distinguished by the number of comment signs or replaced by equal signs or dashes. With the help of the headers, the code could be more logical and readable.
-4 Packages:
We can find useful packages on the website https://cran.r-project.org/mirrors.html and download the package with the needed function. Once we decide on the package, we can also install it in the RStudio packages part or write a command in the console.
https://www.tidyverse.org/packages/
Core tidyverse
The definition of tidy data is that each column is a variable, and each row is an observation.
The core tidyverse includes the packages that you’re likely to use in everyday data analyses. As of tidyverse 1.3.0, the following packages are included in the core tidyverse:
ggplot2
ggplot2 is a system for declaratively creating graphics, based on The Grammar of Graphics. You provide the data, tell ggplot2 how to map variables to aesthetics, and what graphical primitives to use, and it takes care of the details.
dplyr
dplyr provides a grammar of data manipulation, providing a consistent set of verbs that solve the most common data manipulation challenges.
tidyr
tidyr provides a set of functions that help you get to tidy data. Tidy data is data with a consistent form: in brief, every variable goes in a column, and every column is a variable.
readr
readr provides a fast and friendly way to read rectangular data (like csv, tsv, and fwf). It is designed to flexibly parse many types of data found in the wild, while still cleanly failing when data unexpectedly changes.
purrr
purrr enhances R’s functional programming (FP) toolkit by providing a complete and consistent set of tools for working with functions and vectors. Once you master the basic concepts, purrr allows you to replace many for loops with code that is easier to write and more expressive.
tibble
tibble is a modern re-imagining of the data frame, keeping what time has proven to be effective, and throwing out what it has not. Tibbles are data. frames that are lazy and surly: they do less and complain more forcing you to confront problems earlier, typically leading to cleaner, more expressive code.
stringr
stringr provides a cohesive set of functions designed to make working with strings as easy as possible. It is built on top of stringi, which uses the ICU C library to provide fast, correct implementations of common string manipulations.

forcats
forcats provides a suite of useful tools that solve common problems with factors. R uses factors to handle categorical variables, variables that have a fixed and known set of possible values.
-5 Piped Commands with %>%:
Piped commands are usually used in the nested forms of composite functions. They are contained in the dplyr package in R to show a sequence of operations on a data frame. The sequence clearly shows which function is used in which data. The result generated in the previous line will be imported into a later operation.
-6 Import data:
R’s built-in datasets:
The datasets package is installed by default but not loaded, so we also need to run ‘library()’ command to load datasets. To get help on packages, we can enter ?datasets. Library(help=”dataset”) can get the list of datasets.
Explore datasets with pacman:
Pacman, the package manager, can be prepared by ‘if (!require(“pacman”)) install.packages(“pacman”)’ ‘pacman::p_load(datasets, pacman, rio, tidyverse)’
p_data() function is from pacman package, then the function inside can be filled by those packages above. (ggplot2, dplyr, tidyr, stringr, forcats). There are also many packages available such as carData, caret, cluster, DescTools, GGally, gnm,janeaustenr, Lahman, lava, lmtest, maps, MASS, mlbench,nlme, nycflights13, psych, qcc, quantreg, rpart, SixSigma, survival, titanic, vcd
Import data from a spread sheet:
• df1 <- read_csv(“data/StateData.csv”)
• df2 <- import(“data/StateData.xlsx”) %>% as_tibble()
• df3 <- import(“data/StateData.xlsx”) %>%
as_tibble() %>%
select(state_code,
psychRegions,
instagram:modernDance) %>%
mutate(psychRegions = as.factor(psychRegions)) %>%
rename(y = psychRegions) %>%
print()
Import XML data:
Data from the web are hierarchically structured data. XML files are commonly used to store those data, which stands for Extensible Markup Language. In RStudio, we can turn the nested structure into the kind of rectangular data frame.
To begin with, we can download some packages. XML2R is for working with XML data. For example, we can find more information on ergast.com. If we want to download the data from the web, it means the internet should be connected. df stands for data frame.
After a series of transformations, the nested structure is changed to a small data frame, which is more convenient to handle and more intuitive.
In extracting and combing data processes, we can extract data with the select( ) function or filter() and then print them out.
Import JSON data:
It is an older method that stands for JavaScript object notation. The approach to importing data is the same as the previous part of XML.
First, we need to contribute jsonlite with the command pacman::p_load(pacman, tidyverse, jsonlite) and then enter from JSON(). The approaches to locating data are the same as in the previous part.
Saving data in native R formats:
save(df, file = “data/StateData.rdata”)
Saving a file as a format of rdata is much smaller than the common form csv or xlsx. After loading the saved data file, we can choose base Rs’s load() or rio’s import() to read Rdata files.
‘import’ is a command from the ‘rio’ package. It doesn’t display immediately in the environment but shows in the console. We can also save this form of a file by renaming it.

entry.title: R-learning-notes-2
entry.content:
R learning notes 2
Category: R
Text:
-1 Visualizing Data with ggplot2
Ggplot stands for Grammar of Graphics. It separates what is graphed from how it is graphed. The structure of ggplot2 commands can help you address the layered grammar of graphics. It is composed of geom_function (geometric), coordinate_function, and facet_function.
In geom_function part, it contains aes part, which stands for aesthetic elements, stat, and position.
Another form is qplot, which commands for quicker work but with less power and control. If you do not need to modify something, it is a clever idea to use that command.
More information can be found in Ggplot.tidyverse.org, exts.ggplot.tidyverse.org/gallery, RStudio also provides a cheat sheet for beginners to learn the functions of R.
Setting colors in R:
After loading data, we can set the color we want when we draw plots of the data.
By doing the question mark in console in R, we can get a brief introduction of colors provided by R.
If we text colors() we will get list of color names. What’s more, datalac.cc/rcolors shows the colors in a more intuitive way. If we want to enter the name of a color, we can put it into quotes in the barplot command. RGB triplets (0.00-1.00), (0-255), RGB hexcodes, index numbers, either of those ways works to set the color of plots and can turn those pictures more intuitive and interesting.
E.g. barplot(x,col=c colors() [589])
Using color palettes:
Palette() can show us the current colors in R and we can use the current palette by setting col= (valid numbers).
More examples can be found at colorbrewer2.org. We can enter p_load(RColorBrewer). These are extremely useful if we want to show diverging, sequential, or qualitative palettes.
Other palettes such as wesanerson, viridis color palettes, scientific jiournal, and Sci-Fi themed color palettes.
If you want to specify your own palettes, you can create a list and construct the color names in it.
Creating bar charts:
“Tabular format” is simply information presented in the form of a table with rows and columns. Before solving questions with data, we need to convert tables to rows and save this form of the data frame.
1. df <- data_name %>% enter the tabular data
2. as_tibble() %>% convert to tibble with rows
3. uncount(n) %>% convert from summary values
4. mutate_all(as_factor) %>% convert all vars to factors
5. mutate_all(fct_infreq) %>% order by descending factors
6. print() show the results
At last, we can use ggsave() as png and set the width, height, dpi.
More kinds of charts can be realized such as side-by-side bar chart, stacked bar chart, 100% stacked bar chart.
Drawing histograms:
 draw a probability density function
We can have bell curves, skewed distributions, and beta distributions. This can show the variability and the collective distribution.
 draw a histogram for a standard normal distribution.
First, generate random numbers and put them into X. Then draw a histogram by ggplot or qplot and add labels.
 draw a density plot
change the command to geom_density()
Creating box plots:
Box plots are useful if you want to simplify the data or check the presence of outliers.
We can select those types of plots as follows:
 all variables at once:
 draw one variable and generate all groups together
 show one variable but divided by groups (~Species)
The order in the () is important because it can display a plot chart or a box plot.
We can draw dotplot, add jittered w/boxplots, or boxplots.
In ggplot we can add coord_flip() command to flip the coordinates.
Box plots are commonly used to simplify the display of quantitative or measured variables.
Creating scatterplots:
When we prefer to have a look at the associations between quantitative variables.
Using geom_jitter() command can let the chart separate the data points so it helps you to see the overall pattern in a more intuitive way.
If we set size=X.Length, we will get a better comprehension of the association between things.
After geom_smooth(method=lm), we generate a linear regression line in the chart.
geom_density2d() can show the density of the data points.
Creating multiple graphs:
It can help us to display multiple charts at once.
 facet_grid(Species ~ .) divide the data by species and stack them.
. means by this other stuff in front of it
We can easily separate the dots and compare different groups easily.
Creating cluster charts:
Cluster charts take the variables in the data set and then find out the similarities of each case based on those variables. We can construct a fixed number of clusters or hierarchical clusters.
rownames_to_column() because in the tidyverse approach, tibbles don’t have row names.
then select() useful columns
mutate_at() to rescale the chart
While analyzing the data:
 agglomerative: every observation starts out on its own and then they get joined into groups
 divisive: observations start together and then separated out
 hclust: from the built-in statistics functions and agglomerative
 hc stands for hierarchical clusters
 dendrogram means the picture of branches

-2 Wrangling Data
The form of tidy data:
I. column == variable/field/attribute
II. row == case/observation ( no headers or spaces
III. cell == single value encoded with text or numbers
IV. file == one level of observation/abstraction
In untidy data:
Spreadsheets would have some problems, we will meet formulas or refer back to things or some data saved in PDF form. The time series data (data spread in two different directions), XML/JSON data, and data with compound values are also untidy data.
We can turn untidy data into tidy data by generating a new data frame named tidy_ts, meaning to save it as a tsymbol or as a tsibble(time series tibble) then select variables with mutate().
After changing into tidy data, we can easily have further analysis.
Tibbles:
It is a way of storing data and viewing them in a variety of tables. Tibble has an enhanced version of a data frame. Tibbles is a great way of printing and displaying large datasets.
Use class(df) to get the class of df.
Use mutate( Tree= ) to convert it to factors
Using data.table:
Data table is used to work with a large load of data sets.
pacman::p_load(pacman, data.table, httr, tidyverse)

pacman: for loading/unloading packages

data.table: for working with large datasets

httr: for working with web sources

We can filter rows, select columns, or work out both tasks.
Converting the shape of data:
Using the function spread(key=, value= ) to category and split into columns, and set values for each category. This turn tall value to wide.
Function gathers to change the shape of data from wide to tall.
Converting data from tables to rows:
We can turn some compact formats that list categorical variables and combinations to be readable.
After entering the value into a data frame, we can text as_tibble() to convert it to tibble with rows.
Data with times:
If we need to deal with data describing dates, months, years, or many kinds of procedures, we can get help from some packages in R.
pacman::p_load(datasets, lubridate, pacman, tidyverse,
tsibble)

datasets: for demonstration purposes

lubridate: for working with dates

pacman: for loading/unloading packages

tidyverse: for so many reasons

tsibble: for working with time-series data

We can also create separate variables for year, month, and day.
List data:
The list form stores the result of regression if you need it and allows you to have lots of different data types, structures, and lengths. We can integrate lists by creating columns and renaming them to help us to organize the data and then solve problems.
XML data:
If we download some data set on the Internet, we need to use xml2 for working with XML data. XML stands for an extensible markup language.
First, we need to save the URL and then enter the URL into a data set. We can create a tibble to start unnesting the list. (unnest_wider to get the list
We can use summary() to get the statistical features of the dataset.
Categorical variables:
In the mutate() function we can split data by using ifelse() statement and set the condition of test and values if true or false.
If we want to change a character variable into a factor, we need to take a new df and use mutate( A=factor(A)). We can use the function pull() to check the level of the factor.
• Select() returns a data frame
• Pull() returns one variable as a vector
If we want to change the order which is more logical in the real world, we can nest the df with mutate and set levels = c()
with(table())
We can also check the factor frequencies (prop.table()
and turn factors frequencies as proportions (as_tibble().
Filtering cases and subgroups:
We can select individual cases or subgroups in data and focus on their features of them, and extra insight and nuance.
If we want to set the value or select specific features for some variables, we also need two equal signs.
Also, we can combine filters in multiple variables by using logical signs ‘|’,’&’,’!”

entry.title: R-learning-notes-3
entry.content:
R learning notes 3
Category: R
Text:
Review:
unnest() is a function that is used to turn a XML data into a list, it could return unnest_wider or unest_longer.
uncount() performs the opposite to count(), which duplicates rows according to a weighting variable. Although this form is much larger than the original, it is easier to process.

-1 Recoding the data
First, after importing the data, we can enter as_tibble() %>% print() command to check the data type. If it is , we need to define this column as factor: ‘mutate(A=as.factor(A))’
If we don’t need all the data, we can continue to text select() function to keep the data we need.
In the data analysis part, we can use a boxplot of the values to check for outliers or check the frequency of some features.
For categorical variables, we can relevel them to a more intuitive form.
• fct_relevel() is the factor relevel, then we can use ggplot() and draw bar chart of it.
• fct_infreq() : relevel by descending frequencies: the tallest on the left and the smallest on the right. The frequencies are tapering down in the chart, so we can easier to analyze them.
Collapse categories:
• fct_collapse(): to create new levels with selected names
• fct_lump(): collapse categories by rank; n is how many to keep distinct, and then lump and combine all the rest of them.
• fct_lump_min(): collapse by frequencies, min is the setting of n% cutoff.
• fct_other( ,keep=‘ ’) : keep only specified category, other levels are collapsed into a new ‘other’ level.
Remove levels:
We can use filter() to select some levels with logical signs commands.
-2 Recoding quantitative data
• Reorder the data by mathematic operation
• Standardizing values with z-scores: use scale() to transform data to z-scores, normal distribution N(0,1). If the data is standardized, they are all on the same scale. We cannot change the standard deviation, but we can choose what item to display. If scale(, scale=False), it means we only adjust to deviations from the mean.
• Standardizing to other values:
• T-scores: it has a mean of 50 and SD of 10
scale() %>% ‘*’(10) %>% ‘+’(50)
-3 Transform outliers:
We need to know the importance of outliers, what are our options, and the statistical meaning behind them. In order to visualize the data, we can use hist(), and boxplot() (shows the middle range or most data’s compressed distribution). They are commonly used for standard analysis like scatter plots and means.
If we want to focus on the non-outline scores, we can remove the outliers. It depends on how to define the sample and want conclusions we want to draw.
There is another way to bring in outliers called ‘Winsorizing’.
We can also create new groups with mutate( =ifelse() ).
-4 Creating scale scores by counting
• Create new variables to count variables that meet the criterion:
df <- df %>%
mutate(
artsCount = # Create a new variable
ifelse(museum > 1, 1, 0) + # Add 1 if true
ifelse(scrapbook > 1, 1, 0) + # Add 1 if true
ifelse(modernDance > 1, 1, 0) # Add 1 if true
)
• Create categories with the case when:
df %<>%
mutate(
likeArts = case_when(
museum > 1 | scrapbook > 1 | modernDance > 1 ~ “yes”,
TRUE ~ “no” # All other values
)
) %>%
select(
state_code,
likeArts,
artsCount,
museum:modernDance) %>%
arrange(desc(artsCount)) %>%
print(n = Inf)
• Other packages of R-project: psych, Scale also work.
-5 Creating scale scores by averaging scores:
df %<>%
mutate(
artsCrafts = rowMeans(df %>% # Select data
select(museum:modernDance), # Select columns
na.rm = TRUE) # Remove missing values
) %>%
arrange(desc(artsCrafts)) %>% # Put in descending order
print(n = Inf) # Display all rows

entry.title: SQL-questions-1
entry.content:
1. Big Countries
    Table: World
    +————-+———+
    | Column Name | Type |
    +————-+———+
    | name | varchar |
    | continent | varchar |
    | area | int |
    | population | int |
    | gdp | int |

+————-+———+
name is the primary key column for this table.
Each row of this table gives information about the name of a country, the continent to which it belongs, its area, the population, and its GDP value.

A country is big if:
• it has an area of at least three million (i.e., 3000000 km2), or
• it has a population of at least twenty-five million (i.e., 25000000).
Write an SQL query to report the name, population, and area of the big countries.
Return the result table in any order.
The query result format is in the following example.

Example 1:
Input:
World table:
+————-+———–+———+————+————–+
| name | continent | area | population | gdp |
+————-+———–+———+————+————–+
| Afghanistan | Asia | 652230 | 25500100 | 20343000000 |
| Albania | Europe | 28748 | 2831741 | 12960000000 |
| Algeria | Africa | 2381741 | 37100000 | 188681000000 |
| Andorra | Europe | 468 | 78115 | 3712000000 |
| Angola | Africa | 1246700 | 20609294 | 100990000000 |
+————-+———–+———+————+————–+
Output:
+————-+————+———+
| name | population | area |
+————-+————+———+
| Afghanistan | 25500100 | 652230 |
| Algeria | 37100000 | 2381741 |
+————-+————+———+
TARGET:

CODE
SELECT name,population,area
FROM world
WHERE area>=3000000 or population>=25000000;

  1. Recyclable and Low Fat Products
    Table: Products
    +————-+———+
    | Column Name | Type |
    +————-+———+
    | product_id | int |
    | low_fats | enum |
    | recyclable | enum |
    +————-+———+
    product_id is the primary key for this table.
    low_fats is an ENUM of type (‘Y’, ‘N’) where ‘Y’ means this product is low fat and ‘N’ means it is not.
    recyclable is an ENUM of types (‘Y’, ‘N’) where ‘Y’ means this product is recyclable and ‘N’ means it is not.

Write an SQL query to find the ids of products that are both low fat and recyclable.
Return the result table in any order.
The query result format is in the following example.
SELECT product_id
FROM Products
WHERE low_fats=’Y’ AND recyclable=’Y’;

  1. Find Customer Referee
    Table: Customer
    +————-+———+
    | Column Name | Type |
    +————-+———+
    | id | int |

| name | varchar |
| referee_id | int |
+————-+———+
id is the primary key column for this table.
Each row of this table indicates the id of a customer, their name, and the id of the customer who referred them.

Write an SQL query to report the names of the customer that are not referred by the customer with id = 2.
Return the result table in any order.
The query result format is in the following example.

A simple query with easy NULL handling using COALESCE
SELECT name
FROM Customer
WHERE COALESCE(referee_id,0) <> 2;
here COALESCE is used to replace NULL values with zero before checking whether it is equal to 2 or not.

  1. Find Customer Referee
    +————-+———+
    | Column Name | Type |
    +————-+———+
    | id | int |

| name | varchar |
| referee_id | int |
+————-+———+
id is the primary key column for this table.
Each row of this table indicates the id of a customer, their name, and the id of the customer who referred them.

Write an SQL query to report the names of the customer that are not referred by the customer with id = 2.
Return the result table in any order.
The query result format is in the following example.
SELECT name
FROM Customer
WHERE referee_id != 2 OR referee_id IS null;

  1. Customers Who Never Order
    Table: Customers
    +————-+———+
    | Column Name | Type |
    +————-+———+
    | id | int |

| name | varchar |
+————-+———+
id is the primary key column for this table.
Each row of this table indicates the ID and name of a customer.

Table: Orders
+————-+——+
| Column Name | Type |
+————-+——+
| id | int |
| customerId | int |
+————-+——+
id is the primary key column for this table.
customerId is a foreign key of the ID from the Customers table.
Each row of this table indicates the ID of an order and the ID of the customer who ordered it.

Write an SQL query to report all customers who never order anything.
Return the result table in any order.
The query result format is in the following example.
Example 1:
Input:
Customers table:
+—-+——-+
| id | name |
+—-+——-+
| 1 | Joe |
| 2 | Henry |
| 3 | Sam |
| 4 | Max |
+—-+——-+
Orders table:
+—-+————+
| id | customerId |
+—-+————+
| 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 1 |
+—-+————+
Output:
+———–+
| Customers |
+———–+
| Henry |
| Max |
+———–+

select customers.name as ‘Customers’
from Customers
where Customers.id not in (select customerid from Orders);

Summary：
When using ‘where’ command to select, we should be aware of the logical signs and the target to represent. If needed we rename some columns.

SELECT Customers.name AS Customers
FROM Customers
LEFT JOIN Orders
ON Customers.id = Orders.customerId
WHERE Orders.customerId is null;
From：https://leetcode.com/problems/customers-who-never-order/solutions/2620211/easy-joins-with-attached-document/
